document.title: M1 Lattice: Merging Guide
document.purpose: Provides exploratory guidance for combining M1 transport units. This document reflects early understanding and is expected to evolve with practical experience.

core_statement: Merging behavior is not defined by the M1 specification. Applications determine how data is combined, interpreted, or rejected.

status_note.description: This document is exploratory. It captures current thinking and patterns, but is not authoritative. It is expected to evolve as real-world usage reveals better approaches.

data_model_perspective.description: M1 transport units are best understood as partial contributions to a shared, evolving dataset rather than as self-contained documents.
principles: An M1 transport unit is a contribution, not a complete dataset.
Aspects may be understood as tables, keyed by entity identifier.
Entities correspond to rows within those tables.
Aspect contents represent values within those rows.
Multiple transport units may contribute overlapping or conflicting values.
implications: Merging M1 data is a form of database reconciliation, not file merging.
Applications construct their own working views of the dataset.
data_model_perspective.summary: M1 transport units are fragments of a shared database. Merging them requires reconciling overlapping contributions.

separation_of_concerns.description: Different systems play different roles in the handling of M1 data.
roles: Consumers: operate on clean, well-defined data.
principles: Not all applications should implement merging logic.
Conflict resolution is a distinct responsibility.
Consumers may rely on upstream tools to prepare data.

consumer_policy.description: Consumer applications may require data to be free of contradictions.
principles: Applications may reject data that is not trivially mergeable.
Applications are not required to resolve conflicts.
Rejecting ambiguous data is a valid and recommended strategy for deterministic systems.

definition.trivially_mergeable: Data is trivially mergeable if, for any given entity and aspect, multiple values are either absent or identical.

consumer_policy.summary: Consumers may demand clean input and delegate conflict resolution to other tools.

conflict_response_strategies.description: Applications may respond to conflicting data in different ways.
approaches: Select: Choose one value based on criteria.
Partition: Maintain separate views or layers.
Quarantine: Select one value while preserving others for review.
Refuse: Reject or halt processing until conflicts are resolved.
notes: No strategy is mandated by M1.
Different applications may adopt different strategies.
The choice of strategy depends on the application's purpose.

log_centric_model.description: The log aspect is the primary mechanism for understanding change over time.
principles: Structure (entities and aspects) represents current state.
The log represents the history of changes to an entity.
Conflict resolution decisions may be informed by examining log entries.
Time and change should be expressed through logs rather than embedded throughout the data model.
notes: Applications may interpret logs differently depending on their needs.
Logs provide context but do not enforce resolution.

merging_baseline.description: A minimal, safe merging approach may be used as a starting point.
principles: Combine all contributions.
If multiple values exist for the same entity and aspect:
If identical, treat as one.
If different, treat as a conflict.
notes: This baseline avoids silent data loss.
It is intended as a foundation for further strategies.

tooling_expectation.description: Merging and conflict resolution are expected to be supported by dedicated tools.
principles: Tools may detect and classify conflicts.
Tools may assist users in resolving conflicts.
Tools may produce clean, conflict-free datasets for consumption.
Tooling is expected to evolve alongside the ecosystem.
tooling_expectation.summary: The ecosystem is expected to develop specialized tools for reconciliation and preparation of data.

final_note: Merging in M1 is not about determining a single truth. It is about managing overlapping contributions and making explicit decisions about how they are interpreted.
